steps.title: Identify what determines the margin of error
steps.content:
The margin of error depends on three factors: the critical value (z, which is the same for both samples since they used the same method), the sample proportion (p), and the sample size (n).

To find why Sample A's margin is larger, we need to determine which of these factors differs.

steps.title: Rule out the sample proportion as the cause
steps.content:
Calculate $p(1-p)$ for each sample:
  • Sample A: $0.52 \times 0.48 = 0.2496$
  • Sample B: $0.48 \times 0.52 = 0.2496$
The proportions produce identical values, so the percent in favor (52% vs 48%) does not explain the difference in margins of error.

steps.title: Conclude from the inverse relationship with sample size
steps.content:
The margin of error is inversely proportional to the square root of sample size.

A smaller sample size produces a larger margin of error; a larger sample size produces a smaller margin of error.

Since Sample A has a larger MOE (4.2% vs 1.6%), Sample A must have the smaller sample size.

D is correct.
